content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 6, 2004

Glenn Mondo
Weule, Ballard & Mondo, LLP
Tower 17
18881 Von Karman Avenue, Suite 1225
Irvine, CA 92612

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-205

Dear Mr. Mondo:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ On October 5, 2004, you
confirmed you were the subject of the request for advice.

QUESTION

Do any provisions of the Political Reform Act prevent you from participating in a
land use and zoning decision regarding two proposed mixed use developments, including
four high-rise residential towers, when your primary residence is located more than 500
feet from these projects?

CONCLUSION

Your principal residence is located more than 500 feet from the proposed
boundaries of both the Nexus and the Geneva Commons projects. It is presumed under
the Commission's regulations that neither of these decisions will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on the economic interest of your principal residence.
As long as this presumption is not rebutted, you will not have a conflict of interest
concerning either of these decisions, based on your principal residence.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04-205
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the chair of the Santa Ana Planning Commission. Your primary
residence is in the Sandpointe neighborhood, a 1970's era residential development of
single-family homes in the southeast portion of Santa Ana. The Sandpointe
neighborhood consists of approximately 800 residences. The Sandpointe neighborhood is
one of the 52 neighborhoods in the city which are organized to the point of having a
board and representation on the city's Community Linkages, a body consisting of
representatives of each neighborhood. You are not part of the board of directors for the
Sandpointe neighborhood or Community Linkages. In the past, you have provided pro
bono legal services to the Sandpointe neighborhood, regarding its status as a nonprofit
corporation.

The City of Santa Ana has a population exceeding 330,000 and over 70,000
households. Santa Ana is a "built out" city with very little vacant land, to the point that it
is the second densest city in California. Over 60 percent of the city is devoted to
residential uses, including the vast majority of the south end of the city, which is where
the Sandpointe neighborhood is located.

The city's planning commission, by law, recommends approval or denial of major
land use matters to the city council. While commissioners are appointed with a ward
designation (the city has six council member wards and a mayor elected at large), they
are appointed by the council as a whole, are not required to be a resident of their ward,
and essentially serve the city at large.

Two major projects have been proposed in the south part of the City of Santa Ana.
The first is known as the Nexus project, and at its closest spot, it is located approximately
730 feet from your primary residence. The Nexus project is a mixed use project that
currently consists of a proposed 835 mid-rise and high rise residential units and
approximately 13,000 square feet of commercial development. The proposed project
would require amendment of the city's general plan and zoning, as the property is
currently designated commercial.

The second major project is known as Geneva Commons. It is located across the
street from the Nexus project, and at its closest spot, it is approximately 1,400 feet from
your primary residence. It consists of approximately 278 residential units split between
an 18 story and an eight story building, together with ancillary retail.

The city is currently preparing an environmental impact report (EIR) to study the
impacts of the proposed Nexus project. The draft EIR has not been released, but its focus
will include air quality, traffic, light and glare. A mitigated negative declaration is being
proposed for the Geneva Commons project, which would mean that the project, as
mitigated, would not create a significant impact to the environment.

File No. A-04-205
Page No. 3

The exterior boundaries of both the Nexus and Geneva Commons projects are
located well beyond 500 feet of your primary residence. The exterior boundary of the
Sandpointe neighborhood is within 500 feet of the Nexus Project.

ANALYSIS

A public official may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to
use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official
knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)
The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(1) -
(8).)

Steps One and Two: Are you considered a "public official" making, participating in
making, or influencing a governmental decision?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections 87100, 87103; regulation 18700(b)(1).) "Public official" is defined as "every
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency ...."
(Section 82048.) A "local government agency" means a county, city or district of any
kind, including any city board or commission. (Section 82041.) As a planning
commissioner for the City of Santa Ana, you are a "public official" for purposes of the
Act, and the conflict-of-interest rules apply to you.²

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his
or her position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official
negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) When, as a planning commissioner, you
review a project for possible approval, you will be making a governmental decision.

² If a public official's office is listed in section 87200 ("87200 filers" include members of city
planning commissions) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting,
then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify type of
economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in
regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (b)(1)(A)-(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself;
and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions,
consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special
rules found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.) Because you are a
member of the City of Santa Ana Planning Commission, these requirements apply to you.

File No. A-04-205
Page No. 4

Step Three: What are your economic interests — the possible sources of a conflict of interest?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic interests, described as follows:

•	A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment 3 of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));

•	A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2);

•	A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3);

•	A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(e); regulation 18703.4);

•	A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 18703.5).

"Interest in real property" includes:

"…any leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or other filer, or his or her immediate family if the fair market value of the interest is two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. Interests in real property of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real property of any business entity or trust in

_____________
3 An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family" is defined at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.

File No. A-04-205
Page No. 5

which the individual or immediate family owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater."
(Section 82033.)

Presumably, your interest in the home which is your principal residence has a fair market
value of $2,000 or more. Thus, you have an economic interest in your principal
residence.

You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests which
you may have.4 For purposes of this letter, we assume that you have no other economic
interests relevant to the decisions you have identified.

Step Four: Will this economic interest be directly or indirectly involved in decisions
concerning these two projects?

A public official's real property is considered to be "directly involved" in the
decisions listed at subdivisions (a)(2) - (a)(6) of regulation 18704.2 (copy enclosed).
None of these decisions are implicated with respect to your property by the facts you
provide.

In addition, pursuant to regulation 18704.2(a)(1), a public official's interest in real
property is considered directly involved in a decision if any part of the official's real
property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries of the real property which is the
subject of a governmental decision. According to the facts you provide, your residence is
located approximately 730 feet from the Nexus project and 1,400 feet from the Geneva
Commons project.

If the public official's interest in real property is not directly involved in the
governmental decision, it is deemed to be indirectly involved. Thus, your economic
interest in your principal residence will be indirectly involved in the decisions concerning
these two projects.

Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?

Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on indirectly involved real property is presumed not to be material. This
presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in which
the official has an interest. Examples of specific circumstances which will be considered
include, but are not limited to, circumstances where the decision affects:

4 For example, you have not made any reference to an ownership interest in any common property
of the neighborhood, as is the case in some planned unit developments. We assume you have no such
interest in any common areas. If you do have such an interest, the advice will need to be revised.

File No. A-04-205
Page No. 6

"(A) The development potential or income producing
potential of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has
an economic interest;
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy,
intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits
of the neighborhood." (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1)(A-C).)

Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon your economic interest will meet the applicable materiality standard?

An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)

It is presumed that you do not have a conflict of interest arising from your
economic interest in your home. According to the facts provided, decisions concerning
the two projects presumably will not have a material financial effect upon your economic
interest. However, you have stated that it is clear that the Nexus project will change the
area near your home from solely commercial to part commercial and part residential.
Consequently, it appears the decision you have identified with regard to the Nexus
project may result in a material financial effect on your residence by changing the
character of the neighborhood, thereby rebutting the presumption. Because the
Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71), it is ultimately up to you to decide whether your facts are sufficient to
rebut this presumption. If it is not rebutted, you may participate in the decisions.

Step Seven: "Public Generally" Exception

If you determine that the presumption of regulation 18705.2(b)(1) is rebutted, you
may wish to examine whether the "public generally" exception applies.

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate if the "public generally" exception applies. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision upon a public official's economic interests is
indistinguishable from the effect of the decision on the public generally. (Section 87103;
regulation 18707(a).) Under this two-pronged exception, if a "significant segment"
(prong 1) of the public generally is affected by the governmental decision in
"substantially the same manner" (prong 2) as the decision affects the official's economic
interest, the official may participate in the decision.

File No. A-04-205
Page No. 7

Prong 1 -- Significant Segment

Real Property

Specific subdivisions of regulation 18707.1 define what constitutes a "significant
segment." When the economic interest which gives rise to the conflict of interest is an
interest in real property, such as yours, the significant segment is defined as:

"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all
homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or
the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency." (Regulation
18707.1(b)(1)(B)(i) - (ii).)

The facts show that the Nexus project's effects are not localized to the area
immediately adjacent to the project's proposed boundaries. You indicate in your letter
that the Nexus project would arguably affect all residential property owners in the
southern portion of the city, far more than 10 percent of all property owners or all
homeowners in the jurisdiction of your agency. We do not have facts to determine
whether 10 percent of all property owners or homeowners in the city will be affected by
this project. If 10 percent of all property owners or homeowners will be affected by the
project, then the significant segment prong will be met. If not, the exception is not
applicable.

Prong Two -- Substantially the Same Manner

After determining that a significant segment will be affected by a decision, it is
next necessary to evaluate whether the significant segment will be affected by the
decision in "substantially the same manner" as you will be. If the answer is "yes," then
the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
and you may participate in the decision. (Regulation 18707(b)(4).) To assess whether a
significant segment will be financially affected in substantially the same manner, all
measurable effects from the decision must be identified. Without additional facts, we are
unable to determine whether this exception applies.

Step Eight: "Legally Required Participation" Exception

The "legally required participation" exception permits an official who is
otherwise disqualified from making a governmental decision to participate in the decision
when the official's participation is legally required. (Section 87101; regulation 18708.)
This exception (step eight of our standard conflict-of-interest analysis) is narrowly
construed and applies only when it is legally impossible for the decision to be made
without the participation of a disqualified official. Typically, this exception is invoked
when, due to disqualification, an agency is unable to convene a quorum of its members.

In your case, have you have not presented facts to indicate that there is no

File No. A-04-205
Page No. 8

alternate source of decision. Since this exception is narrowly construed and applies only
when a decision is legally impossible to make, it is unlikely that your participation as
planning commissioner is legally required.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

[signature: Groh Mayer]

By: Trish Mayer
Political Reform Consultant

homeowners in the jurisdiction of your agency. We do not have facts to determine
whether 10 percent of all property owners or homeowners in the city will be affected by
this project. If 10 percent of all propert5r owners or homeowners will be affected by the
project, then the significant segment prong will be met. Ifnot, the exception is not
applicable.
Prong Two - Substantially the Same Manner
After deterrnining that a significant segment will be affected by a decision, it is
next necessary to evaluate whether the significant segment will be afFected by the
decision in "substantially the same mannef' as you will be. If the answer is 'lres," then
the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from the effect on thepublic generally
and you may participate in the decision. (Regulation 18707(b)(a).) To assess whether a
significant segment will be financially affected in substantially the same manner, all
measurable effects from the decision must be identified. V/ithout additional facts, we are
unable to determine whether this exception applies.
Step Eight: 'ol,egally Required Participation, Exception
The "legally required participation" exception penrrits an official who is
otherwise disqualified from making a govemmental decision to participate in the decision
when the official's participation is legally required. (Section 87101; regulation 18703.)
This exception (step eight of our standard conflict-of-interest analysis) is narrowly
construed and applies only when it is legally impossible for the decision to be made
without the participation of a disqualified ofiEcial. Typically, this exception is invoked
when, due to disqualification, an agency is unable to convene a quonrm of its members.
In your case, have you have not presented facts to indicate that there is no
alternate source of decision. Since this exception is narrowly construed and applies only
when a decision is legally impossible to make, it is unlikely that yourparticipation as
planning commissioner is legally required.

QUESTION: Do anyprovisions of the Political Reform Act prevent you from participating in a
land use and zoning decision regarding two proposed mixed use developments, including
four high-rise residential towers, when your primary residence is located more than 500
feet from these projects?

CONCLUSION: Your principal residence is located more than 500 feet from the proposed
boundaries of both the Nexus and the Geneva Commons projects. It is presumed under
the Commission's regulations that neither of these decisions will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on the economic interest of yourprincipal residence.
As long as this presumption is not rebutted, you will not have a conflict of interest
conceming either of these decisions, based on your principal residence.
FileNo. A-04-205
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 October 6, 2004 Glenn Mondo Weule, Ballard & Mondo, LLP Tower 17 18881 Von Karman Avenue, Suite 1225 Irvine, CA 92612 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-04-205 Dear Mr. Mondo: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ On October 5, 2004, you confirmed you were the subject of the request for advice. QUESTION Do any provisions of the Political Reform Act prevent you from participating in a land use and zoning decision regarding two proposed mixed use developments, including four high-rise residential towers, when your primary residence is located more than 500 feet from these projects? CONCLUSION Your principal residence is located more than 500 feet from the proposed boundaries of both the Nexus and the Geneva Commons projects. It is presumed under the Commission's regulations that neither of these decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the economic interest of your principal residence. As long as this presumption is not rebutted, you will not have a conflict of interest concerning either of these decisions, based on your principal residence. ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-04-205 Page No. 2 FACTS You are the chair of the Santa Ana Planning Commission. Your primary residence is in the Sandpointe neighborhood, a 1970's era residential development of single-family homes in the southeast portion of Santa Ana. The Sandpointe neighborhood consists of approximately 800 residences. The Sandpointe neighborhood is one of the 52 neighborhoods in the city which are organized to the point of having a board and representation on the city's Community Linkages, a body consisting of representatives of each neighborhood. You are not part of the board of directors for the Sandpointe neighborhood or Community Linkages. In the past, you have provided pro bono legal services to the Sandpointe neighborhood, regarding its status as a nonprofit corporation. The City of Santa Ana has a population exceeding 330,000 and over 70,000 households. Santa Ana is a "built out" city with very little vacant land, to the point that it is the second densest city in California. Over 60 percent of the city is devoted to residential uses, including the vast majority of the south end of the city, which is where the Sandpointe neighborhood is located. The city's planning commission, by law, recommends approval or denial of major land use matters to the city council. While commissioners are appointed with a ward designation (the city has six council member wards and a mayor elected at large), they are appointed by the council as a whole, are not required to be a resident of their ward, and essentially serve the city at large.
